coredata.dc:title: Lysosome-targeted red-fluorescent carbon dots for turn-on detection of permanganate and pH in vivo and in vitro
coredata.dc:creator: Dan Chang,
Zhao, Zhonghua
Feng, Jianyang
Xin, Yuye
Yang, Yongxing
Shi, Lihong
coredata.dc:description: Rapid, reliable, and convenient fluorescent probes are of great importance for specifically identifying permanganate and pH in biological systems. In this work, raw materials were provided by malic acid and neutral red for the preparation of red emission carbon dots (RCDs) using a hydrothermal carbonization approach. Superior features of RCDs including good stability, exceptional biocompatibility, and prominent water solubility were found, which enables them to favorably track permanganate and pH. Upon the introduction of permanganate, as-acquired RCDs presented a notable emission enhancement owing to aggregation induced emission (AIE) mechanism. Results indicated that they can act as “turn-on” fluorescent sensor for quantitative analysis of permanganate in the scope of 0–115 μM with a detection limit of 14.5 nM. Most importantly, this probe exhibited a special responsive functionality towards pH, in which a good linearity in the pH range of 6.2–8.2 could be readily realized. Besides, evident color changes of RCDs solution under sunlight can be visualized at different pH, which is more desirable for the fluorescent and colorimetric bimodal sensing of pH. Intriguingly, it was observed that RCDs can successfully target lysosome in living cells. Finally, as-developed RCDs were performed for the label-free, real-time visualization of permanganate and pH fluctuations in vivo and in vitro.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red fluorescence
Carbon dots
MnO4 −
PH
Lysosome
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence determination of MnO4 − and pH
Fluorescent and colorimetric detection of pH

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are grateful for the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21874087 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the synthesis of RCDs.
(A) UV–vis absorption of RCDs. (B) Excitation-emission spectra of RCDs. (λex=370−430 nm).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of RCDs after introducing various concentrations of MnO4 −. (0−115 μM). (B) Linear relationship of ΔF and MnO4 − concentrations. (ΔF=(F-F0), F0 and F represent the FL intensity of RCDs without and with MnO4 −).

(A) Fluorescence response of RCDs over pH. (2−12) (B) Relationship between the PL intensity and pH. (C) Linear relationship of the PL intensity versus pH. (D) Reversible fluorescence response between 6.2 and 8.2. (E) Photographs of RCDs solution at different pH values under sunlight.
Working mechanism of RCDs for pH sensing.
Cell images of RCDs responds to MnO4 − in HeLa cells. (0–10 min).
Cell images of RCDs at different pH values in HeLa cells.
Intracellular localization of RCDs in HeLa cells.
Images of RCDs responds to MnO4 − in zebrafish larvae.
Images of RCDs at different pH values in zebrafish larvae.

head.ce:title: Lysosome-targeted red-fluorescent carbon dots for turn-on detection of permanganate and pH in vivo and in vitro

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Biomedical Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Biomedical Science, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Biomedical Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid, reliable, and convenient fluorescent probes are of great importance for specifically identifying permanganate and pH in biological systems. In this work, raw materials were provided by malic acid and neutral red for the preparation of red emission carbon dots (RCDs) using a hydrothermal carbonization approach. Superior features of RCDs including good stability, exceptional biocompatibility, and prominent water solubility were found, which enables them to favorably track permanganate and pH. Upon the introduction of permanganate, as-acquired RCDs presented a notable emission enhancement owing to aggregation induced emission (AIE) mechanism. Results indicated that they can act as “turn-on” fluorescent sensor for quantitative analysis of permanganate in the scope of 0–115 μM with a detection limit of 14.5 nM. Most importantly, this probe exhibited a special responsive functionality towards pH, in which a good linearity in the pH range of 6.2–8.2 could be readily realized. Besides, evident color changes of RCDs solution under sunlight can be visualized at different pH, which is more desirable for the fluorescent and colorimetric bimodal sensing of pH. Intriguingly, it was observed that RCDs can successfully target lysosome in living cells. Finally, as-developed RCDs were performed for the label-free, real-time visualization of permanganate and pH fluctuations in vivo and in vitro.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), an emerging star among the nanocarbon family, have unique features in comparison to mickle fluorescent nanomaterials, such as super fluorescence property, excellent solubility, nontoxicity, and eminent biocompatibility. These merits of CDs endow them versatile functions in the fields of photocatalysts [1], energy conversion [2], drug delivery [3], sensors [4], bioimaging [5], and so on. As is well known, a series of investigations utilizing CDs as fluorescent sensors have been frequently reported for the recognition and marking of multiple specific analytes, such as ions, biomolecules, cells, and bacteria [6–9], in which multi-mode fluorescent sensing platforms were designed to improve quality of detection. Surely, fluorescent probes based on CDs for timely tracking and imaging are of considerable significance for thoroughly comprehending cellular functions and giving vital supports in terms of early diagnosis of diseases, which provides an effective and prompt determination platform that can identify target analyte signals. Up to date, lots of fluorescent sensors based on CDs have been developed. For instance, Shuang’s group [10] constructed a dual-responsive CDs-based fluorescent sensor for the ratiometric recognition of vitamin B2 via fluorescence resonance energy transfer, and for the analysis of pH by a “turn-off” single-emission manner. Meng et al. [11] developed that CDs with green fluorescent emission were implemented as the specific “off-on” florescent sensing platform to monitor chromium and ascorbic acid which have smoothly achieved bioimaging for tracking chromium and ascorbic acid levels in living cells. Very recently, Nemati et al. [12] employed citric acid coupled with sodium thiosulfate as raw materials to synthesize CDs directly by microwave-assisted treatment, and obtained CDs showed high sensitivity to Cu2+ and caffeine, which gave us new inspiration. Nonetheless, these fluorescent sensors still face challenges, which mainly stemmed from the limits of the short-wavelength emission CDs in their biological applications. In light of the above facts, great efforts are very valuable for the development of CDs with long-wavelength emission, while still maintaining the excellent optical properties, stability, and biocompatibility.

Permanganate (MnO4 −), well known as a strong oxidizing ion, have been extensively implemented in drinking water and sewage treatment for the removal of turbidity, algae, organic pollutants, and so forth. Studies show that when encountering organic compounds, it can release renascent oxygen promptly, which plays a crucial role in killing bacteria through oxidizing the active genes [13]. On the other hand, aberrant levels of MnO4 − can trigger adverse influences and thus induce different severe diseases, such as gastrointestinal distress, hemorrhage in the esophagus, respiratory damage, and so forth, which are harmful to human health [14,15]. With these factors in mind, the development of reliable, rapid, and precise sensors for monitoring MnO4 − levels is very necessary. To our knowledge, quite a few efforts have been made with regard to the analysis of MnO4 −. For instance, Ye et al. [16] designed glucose-protected GNPs@Ag core−shell nanoparticles as fluorescent probe for single-particle colorimetric recognition of MnO4 −, which resulted from the selective etching of the Ag shell of the GNPs@Ag by MnO4 −. Nosuhi et al. [17] proposed indirect voltammetric determination method of trace amounts of MnO4 − at a zeolite modified carbon paste electrode. Wu et al. [18] developed Eu (III)-functionalized In-MOF (In(OH)bpydc) with prominent optical properties which can serve as florescent nanosensor to monitor MnO4 − via quenching mechanism. Notably, lots of shortcomings concerning these reported sensors were also found, such as complex/sophisticated process, high costs, and tedious sample pretreatment, which hampered its use in practical analysis. In addition, most of fluorescent probes still relied on quenching mechanism. Thus, the reliable and convenient fluorescent probes with simple synthesis are highly desired for quantification of MnO4 − with the “turn-on” detection mode.

Aggregation-induced emission (AIE), as a novel florescent phenomenon, which has sparked great interests of researchers and opens a new avenue for the achievement of fluorescent biosensors. Tang’s group in 2001 reported for the first time that some AIE fluorophores emitted non- or extremely weak emission in the solution state, but they were converted into strong luminescence at the state of aggregation owing to restriction of intramolecular motion mechanism [19]. In detail, rotational and vibrational motions of some molecules in the aggregate state suppressed the non-radiative path and activated radiative decay, eventually causing the AIE behavior [20]. Indeed, different series of fluorescent molecules have been widely developed which were regarded as outstanding candidates for fluorescent sensors in biological researches. However, CDs-based AIE fluorescent sensors are very rare. For this goal, it is still expected to construct ideal fluorescent sensors based on CDs with AIE effect for the valid and efficient recognition of MnO4 −.

Intracellular pH is a cardinal regulative factor of various physiological as well as biochemical processes, such as ATP synthesis, ion transport, cell cycle and apoptosis, DNA replication, cell metabolism, and so on [21]. In biological systems, it modulates lots of crucial intracellular activities of enzymes and metabolic reactions, and regulates the structure and function of biologically active macromolecules [22]. Abnormal changes of pH will affect cell functions and give rise to serious diseases [23]. Thus, measuring pH in living cells is fundamentally significant for maintaining homeostasis and it is urgently needed to develop pH sensors with colorimetric and fluorescent dual mode detection method.

Herein, we reported the synthesis of RCDs using malic acid and neutral red as raw materials ( Fig. 1) and designed a rapid and accurate multifunctional fluorescence sensor for ‘‘turn-on” recognition of MnO4 − and pH both in vitro and in vivo. Compared with those previously reported fluorescent sensors, the advantages of this novel sensor include the following: (1) Multifunctional fluorescence sensor with simple preparation procedure and cheap raw materials was ingeniously developed which does not demand the attachment of additional fluorescent dyes to the nanoparticles and avoids complicated synthesis steps. (2) Label-free and rapid visualization of permanganate and pH can be achieved at the cellular and in vivo levels by virtue of low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and favorable optical property of RCDs. (3) The sensor can be utilized for the precise measurement of pH with a colorimetric and fluorescent bimodal discrimination method in aqueous solution. (4) The sensor holds good targeting effect on lysosome, providing a promising tool for biological and medical areas.

RCDs were obtained with the following processes: 0.1 g malic acid and 0.05 g neutral red were dispersed in ultrapure water (30 mL). Then, the mixture in an autoclave was heated up to 180 °C by maintaining for 3 h. After the reaction, the above dispersions were centrifuged (3000 rpm, 1 h) and filtered by a 0.22 µm microporous membrane, which were further dialyzed with a dialysis membrane for 18 h. At last, these products were dried in a vacuum drying oven for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence determination of MnO4 − and pH
ce:section.ce:para: At first, the titration experiments of RCDs towards MnO4 − and pH were conducted into PBS solution. Specifically, 50 µL RCDs (0.21 mg/mL) aqueous solution were diluted to 500 µL PBS solution (pH=7). After this, various amounts of MnO4 − were introduced into stock solution of RCDs for 30 s. Upon the identical conditions, other competitive analytes including Al3+, Co2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, NO3 −, NO2 −, SCN−, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, CO3 2−, S2−, S2O3 2−, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, and PO4 3− were performed instead of MnO4 −. Their fluorescence spectra were recorded under excitation at 400 nm.
For measurement of pH, 50 µL RCDs (0.21 mg/mL) aqueous solution were diluted to 500 µL PBS solution under different pH conditions. Likewise, the PL spectra were recorded under excitation at 400 nm.
Biological imaging experiments in vivo and in vitro were described in detail in the Supplementary Materials.

The surface status information of RCDs was further analyzed with the help of FT-IR. It is clearly seen (Fig. S18) that absorption peaks of RCDs at around 1020 cm−1/1190 cm−1, 1468 cm−1, 1602 cm−1, and 2916 cm−1 result from the stretching vibrations of C–O, C–N, CO, and C–H. A peak at about 1328 cm−1 is assigned to the bending vibration of C–H. The broad absorption bands at 3067−3464 cm−1 arise from the stretching vibrations of O−H/N−H. Likewise, XPS measurement was executed to assess more detailed chemical components of RCDs. The full-range XPS spectrum (Fig. S1A) identifies that as-acquired RCDs comprise C, N, and O elements. The C1s spectrum (Fig. S1B) presents two noticeable bands: CC (284.91 eV) and OC−O (288.19 eV). The O1s peaks of RCDs at 530.54 eV, 531.99 eV, and 534.07 eV in Fig. S1C confirm that oxygen mainly exists in the forms of C−O, CO, and C−O−C/C−OH. As for the N1s spectrum of RCDs, two binding energies at 399.01 eV and 399.37 eV in Fig. S1D can be related to the amino N and pyridine N, respectively. By statistics, we can exactly infer that as-prepared RCDs have π-conjugated domains in their carbon cores and RCDs surface is rich in abundant functional groups, such as −OH, −COOH, and −NH2, which are conducive to improve water-solubility and fluorescence of RCDs. As far as we know, the surface of RCDs has different kinds of functional groups, which can form many “surface states” energy levels and further induce many emissive traps. When RCDs is illuminated upon a certain excitation wavelength, a surface state emissive trap will dominate the emission. That is, the optical properties of RCDs depend on the competition among different emission traps [24]. Like most reported CDs, result indicates obviously that a broad diffraction peak at around 26° is observed in Fig. S1E, which implies the graphite-like structure of RCDs.

Next, the size and morphology of RCDs were observed using TEM together with AFM. It is also noteworthy from Fig. S2 that RCDs present the homogeneous spherical characteristic and the mean particle size is 2.28 ± 0.17 nm. In detail, the corresponding AFM images (Fig. S3) evidently indicate a typical topographic height of 4.52 nm. Especially, Fig. S4 illustrates that the hydrodynamic diameter of RCDs is approximately 3.43 nm, which validates the above consequences of TEM and AFM.

Also, the corresponding UV–visible absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of RCDs were studied. In Fig. 2 A, the sharp peak at 273 nm could be responsible for the π-π* transition of CC, while the strong peak at 528 nm was related to the n-π * of CO/C−N [25–27]. Fig. 2B displayed the FL spectra of as-obtained RCDs, and it was clear that the optimal emission centered at 627 nm upon an excitation of 400 nm. To the best of our knowledge, most of fluorescent CDs can be affected by numerous factors (ionic strengths, metal ions, and pH) in a complex biological environment. Thus, it is imperative to assess the fluorescence stability of RCDs. [28,29] Initially, we researched the stability of RCDs at different ionic strengths (expressed as NaCl). Virtually no effect for the fluorescence signals of RCDs was observed even at a high concentration of 0.5 M NaCl (Fig. S5A), which proved that as-prepared RCDs can resist comparatively high ionic strength and is beneficial to its application in physiological salt environments. [30] More importantly, since the human body is an electrolyte environment, such result endows their further use in biological application. [31] In addition, it was found in Fig. S5B that there remained constant after storage of RCDs for 600 h in a refrigerator, and no solution agglomeration was seen. Further, this observation manifested from Fig. S5C that the fluorescence intensity of RCDs slightly decreased even after storage for 2 months, revealing that RCDs hold superior long-term stability. On a whole, as-synthesized RCDs possess good stability, which endows them great potentials for biological sample detection and imaging researches in on-site and long duration. The QY of RCDs was 24.9% with reference to Rhodamine B.

Due to abundant active functional groups on the surface of RCDs, we addressed whether as-acquired RCDs could function as a suitable sensor to detect analyte. In further experiments, as we designed, the probe responses to diverse substances were performed. Upon the addition of these analytes, none of them could induce significant changes at 627 nm as shown in Fig. S6, while the relevant fluorescence enhancement was only caused by MnO4 −, evidently implying that as-acquired RCDs could act as a convenient and sensitive “turn-on” platform for monitoring MnO4 −. Increasing MnO4 − concentrations from 0 μM to 115 μM in Fig. 3 A, the emission intensities of RCDs drastically enhanced. Importantly, linearity is good (R2 =0.998) between ΔF and MnO4 − concentrations in the scope of 0−115 μM, and the detection limit is 14.5 nM. These results indicated that as-acquired RCDs have a high sensitivity towards MnO4 − and can be applied for quantification of MnO4 − with the “turn-on” detection mode.

The mechanism behind the probe response to MnO4 − was investigated by means of TEM, AFM, DLS, UV−vis absorption, and fluorescence lifetime. As shown in Fig. S7, bigger size of 7.02 nm was observed following the addition of MnO4 −, which gave direct information on the aggregation of RCDs. Besides, AFM images (Fig. S8) confirmed a similar aggregation behavior. As expected in Fig. S9, the hydrated particle size also increased upon the introduction of MnO4 −, offering strong support that as-acquired RCDs aggregated and assembled in an orderly fashion into large nanoparticles. Further, the absorbance spectra of RCDs in the absence and presence of MnO4 − (Fig. S10) were monitored. It was seen that the absorption spectrum of RCDs increased in the same spectrum pattern upon introducing MnO4 −, and the corresponding explanation may be due to the aggregation of RCDs particles which can absorb and scatter the incident light source. [26] More important evidence was presented in Fig. S11 that the fluorescence lifetimes before and after MnO4 − had no obvious variation from 0.90 ns to 0.95 ns, proving that electron recombination pathway of RCDs was unchanged and the fluorescent emission came from the same fluoresce species but with the enhanced emission efficiency. [32] The consequences implied the increase of the fluorescent emission was associated with restriction of intramolecular motion of the same fluoresce species. [32,33] Based on all the above results and enlightened by previous reports, we deduced that the interaction effect behind the probe response towards MnO4 − supported AIE mechanism. According to lots of reports, aggregation of fluorescent nanoparticles usually depends on the existing functional groups in the aggregation moieties. [34] Thus, we speculated the possible interaction mechanism between RCDs and permanganate (Fig. S12). To be specific, the hypothesis is that in the existence of MnO4 −, the functional groups (–COOH, –OH, or –NH2) on the surface of RCDs formed strong intermolecular hydrogen bonds with O of permanganate to induce the aggregation of RCDs, which enhanced the π-conjugation and restricted the rotation of the functional groups on the surface of RCDs, leading to the enhancement in radiative transition rate and the reduction in non-radiative transition rate. [35–37] That is to say, the fluorescence enhancement of RCDs is due to aggregation state suppressed the non-radiative decay. In short, these evidences strongly validated that permanganate induces the aggregation of RCDs and results in the increase of the fluorescent intensity of RCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent and colorimetric detection of pH
ce:section.ce:para: The response of pH on emission intensity of RCDs was investigated at a broad pH range of 2.0–12.0 upon the excitation wavelength of 400 nm. It was distinct in Fig. 4 A that accompanied by variations of pH, the emission intensities of RCDs at 627 nm enhanced continuously, indicating that as-prepared RCDs are highly pH-sensitive. From Fig. 4B, the intensities increased incrementally by varying the pH values of the medium, while the emission intensities of RCDs showed minimal changes or tended to be gentle under strong acid and alkali conditions. Distinctly, a good linear relationship (R2 =0.993) could be observed from Fig. 4 C in the pH range of 6.2–8.2. Importantly, the fluorescence responses of RCDs were highly reversible as the pH was cycled between 6.2 and 8.2 as shown in Fig. 4D. Notably, the evident color variations of RCDs solution (Fig. 4E) were easily discernable under sunlight with the gradual increase of pH by naked eyes, which indicated that obtained RCDs can be implemented as a convenient and fast sensor for identifying pH values succinctly and intuitively under sunlight without the aid of an ultraviolet (UV) lamp. In terms of the complicated media environments of actual life, the anti-interference capability was further explored. No remarkable emission changes after adding other species can be visualized as displayed in Fig. S13, revealing that RCDs hold fantastic chemical stability and superior specificity towards pH. As we designed, as-acquired RCDs can be applied for pH discrimination in aqueous solution with fluorescence and colorimetric manner. As far as we know, as-prepared RCDs are sensitive to pH, which may be due to the special feature of the functional groups on the surface of RCDs. Based on a very large number of published reports, it has been well proved that some fluorescent CDs are rich in the hydrophilic functional groups (–COOH, –OH, and –NH2), which denoted that CDs are amphoteric and analogous to the properties of amino acids. This feature makes these hydrophilic functional groups easier to be protonated and deprotonated, which is responsible for their high specificity to pH. [38–41].

Next, we deeply explored the pH sensing mechanism of RCDs. As can be seen in Fig. S14, the hydrated particle sizes of RCDs are 3.32 nm and 7.87 nm at pH 3 and 9, respectively. Compared with the hydrated particle size of RCDs (3.43 nm) at pH 7 (Fig. S4), it was found that there was no obvious change in acid solution, but the particle size increased obviously at alkaline condition. Meanwhile, TEM (Fig. S15) and AFM images (Fig. S16) also revealed that the particle size of RCDs at pH 9 is bigger significantly than that at pH 7, providing the reliable evidences that the quasi-spherical RCDs aggregated together at alkaline condition. To further understand the reason for the aggregation of RCDs, we investigated the zeta potential of RCDs at different pH values. The zeta potential of RCDs aqueous solution is −10.4 mV at pH 7 (Fig. S17), which indicated that the surface of RCDs has a certain negative charge and stem from the partial ionization of abundant carboxylic and hydroxyl groups on the surface of RCDs. As the pH value changed from 7 to 3, the result showed that the zeta potential of RCDs aqueous solution increased from −10.4 mV to +1.57 mV, demonstrating that the hydrophilic functional groups (–COOH, –OH, and –NH2) on the surface of RCDs in the rigid carbon skeleton were protonated at lower pH values. As pH value reached 9, the sharp increase of zeta potential can be distinctly observed which confirmed the consumption of –COOH/–OH on the surface of RCDs to interrupt the deprotonation process and the presence of the intermolecular hydrogen bonds among the functional groups (–COOH, –OH or −NH2) of RCDs at alkaline condition. [42] Moreover, this observation (Fig. S18) showed that the stretching vibration absorption bands of −OH/−NH dramatically weakened at pH 9. On the basis of all the above evidences, we concluded that as-prepared RCDs were dissolved as isolated species in acid aqueous solution. On the contrary, the deprotonation process in alkaline solution made the electron-donating abilities of oxygen enhance and induced the interaction of intermolecular hydrogen bonds among RCDs ( Fig. 5), which further resulted in the aggregation of RCDs and the luminescent enhancement. [43–45] In a word, the pH sensing mechanism of RCDs can be attributed to the aggregation-induced enhancing under alkaline conditions.

Excellent biocompatibility of developed nanoprobe is extremely essential for its further practical applications, especially in vitro imaging, so the related cytotoxicity tests of RCDs were determined using HeLa cells at different concentrations. As presented in Fig. S19A, the cell viability of ca. 95% at 10 μg/mL RCDs proved minimal cytotoxicity of probe to the cultured cells. Fig. 6 illustrated that HeLa cells emitted weak fluorescent emission in the red channel after being incubated with RCDs (2 μg/mL) for 15 min. Followed by the introduction of MnO4 − (2 μM), an evident fluorescence enhancement occurred within 10 min, which verified the ability of as-developed probe to track MnO4 − levels in living cells. On the other hand, HeLa cells tagged with RCDs (4 μg/mL) were further incubated by PBS buffer at pH 6, 7.0, 7.5, and 8.0, followed by performing confocal fluorescence imaging. Surely, along with increase of extracellular pH values, the fluorescence intensities of HeLa cells also gradually increased as revealed in Fig. 7. These observations showed that RCDs can function as an ideal probe for label-free detection of intracellular pH and MnO4 − fluctuations. Furthermore, the lysosome-targeting ability of RCDs was evaluated by colocalization experiments using the commercial lysosome dye. As observed in Fig. 8, RCDs (4 μg/mL) in HeLa cells have terrific overlap with Lyso Tracker Green probe, and the colocalization coefficient is 0.92, implying the outstanding ability of RCDs to target lysosome.

Zebrafish larvae was selected as an animal model to validate the feasibility of RCDs for MnO4 − sensing and pH detection in vivo. There’s no doubt that the survivability of zebrafish larvae exposed to RCDs was preliminarily assessed prior to fluorescent imaging in vivo. At the amount of 0.5 mg/mL RCDs, zebrafish larvae expressed a small variation in numbers in Fig. S19B, which proved that as-acquired RCDs have little effects on the growth state of zebrafish larvae and are superior candidates for application in vivo system such as real-time bioimaging as well as molecular tracking. As we expected, zebrafish larvae tagged with RCDs (0.5 mg/mL) could be clearly seen as a slight red fluorescent signal ( Fig. 9). On the contrary, zebrafish larvae tagged with RCDs after the introduction of MnO4 − (1 mM), the fluorescent signal increased. Intriguingly, with the increase of the pH values, an obvious enhancement for fluorescent emission in zebrafish larvae tagged with RCDs (0.5 mg/mL) can be visualized as depicted in Fig. 10. Satisfying results showed that as-prepared RCDs can be act as the multifunctional sensing platform for timely monitoring MnO4 − and pH dynamics in vivo.

In brief, we designed a novel multifunctional sensor based on RCDs for sensing MnO4 − and pH. Using malic acid and neutral red as reaction sources, RCDs with long emission were simply obtained by a one-pot hydrothermal procedure. Results indicated that because of the AIE principle, quantitative detection of MnO4 − levels could be readily achieved by as-developed RCDs. Besides, obtained RCDs were implemented to sense pH in colorimetric and fluorescent bimodal manner. Surprisingly, as-acquired RCDs can favorably target lysosome in living cells. Features of RCDs including good stability, high biocompatibility as well as low toxicity endow them applications for the detection of MnO4 − and pH in vivo and in vitro. This work proved that CDs-based probes are powerful tools and can be applied as ideal candidates for MnO4 − and pH sensing in vivo and in vitro, and for other applications in biological and medical areas.

Dan Chang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Zhonghua Zhao: Investigation, Validation. Jianyang Feng: Investigation, Validation. Yuye Xin: Investigation, Validation. Yongxing Yang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Lihong Shi: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874087).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2021.130774.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visible-light-driven hydrogen evolution using nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot-implanted polymer dots as metal-free photocatalysts
Appl. Catal. B: Environ.

Chou, Visible-light-driven hydrogen evolution using nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot-implanted polymer dots as metal-free photocatalysts, Applied Catalysis B: Environmental 283 (2021) 119659.

J. Drug Deliv. Sci. Technol.

Nanoscale

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dan Chang obtained B.Sc. degree in Chemistry from Xinzhou Teachers University in 2017. Now she is a postgraduate in Shanxi University and major in physical chemistry.

Zhonghua Zhao is now working as a professor in Shanxi University, Shanxi province, China. He obtained his Ph.D degree in 2010. His current research interests include human disease genetics and animal model.
Jianyang Feng is now a postgraduate in Shanxi University and major in materials and chemical engineering.
Yuye Xin is now a postgraduate in Shanxi University and major in microbiology.

Yongxing Yang is now working as a professor in Shanxi University, Shanxi Province, China. He obtained his Ph.D. degree from Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2008. His current research interests include material chemistry and industrial catalysis.

Lihong Shi is now working as a professor in Shanxi University, Shanxi Province, China. She obtained her Ph.D. degree in Physical Chemistry from Shanxi Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2008. Her current research interests include nanomaterial and chemosensors.
